answer:
**Andrew (*Andreas*) both inflicts and endures violence, staging a heroic rescue amid cannibalistic persecution.** He supernaturally slays seven prison guards ("Sudden death took them all, / they fell, fame-less") to free Matthew and other captives,[^1] and later unleashes a divinely commanded flood that drowns many heathens while swallowing fourteen of the "worst" into the earth.[^2] Yet he withholds retaliation during his own torments: dragged through streets, repeatedly scourged over three days until "sweat welled in waves / through his bone-cage," beaten until evening, and confined in winter cold, his body "sorely broken" and "blood-stained," all without counter-violence.[^3] Miraculous healing reinforces his non-retaliatory endurance.[^4]

**Elene (*Elene*), as imperial questor, primarily gives threats and coercion but faces no direct violence.** She terrorizes Jewish elders with pyre-threats ("on a hill a pyre shall consume you") until they yield Judas,[^5] and imprisons him in a dry well, "hunger-haunted, / clasped by fetters" for seven days to extract the True Cross's location.[^6] Judas endures this "torment too hard," pleading release. Elene withholds no personal suffering—her faith-motivated search succeeds through power, not martyrdom.

**Juliana (*Juliana*) purely withholds, embodying passive martyrdom.** Betrothed to pagan Eleusius, she endures scourging ("stripped naked and scourged / with whips"), suspension by hair for six hours under blows, dungeon isolation, boiling lead (emerging unscathed amid 75 deaths), and beheading, all without retaliation.[^7] She spiritually binds and interrogates a demon but inflicts no physical violence on humans, converting onlookers through steadfastness.[^8]

In sum, Andrew balances divine offense with endurance; Elene weaponizes authority offensively; Juliana models total non-violence.[^9]

[^1]: *Andreas*, ch. 1, [p-74e29a](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-74e29a).

[^2]: *Andreas*, ch. 1, [p-e5ccfa](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-e5ccfa); [p-839aab](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-839aab).

[^3]: *Andreas*, ch. 1, [p-34b202](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-34b202); [p-5d78fc](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-5d78fc); [p-17921e](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-17921e).

[^4]: *Andreas*, ch. 1, [p-997c80](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-997c80).

[^5]: *Elene*, ch. 1, [p-f5d1f1](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-f5d1f1).

[^6]: *Elene*, ch. 1, [p-f5d1f1](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-f5d1f1).

[^7]: *Juliana*, ch. 1, [p-7cffd3](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-7cffd3); [p-89a6c9](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-89a6c9); [p-3a01e6](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-3a01e6); [p-7fd33e](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-7fd33e).

[^8]: *Juliana*, ch. 1, [p-bd327c](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-bd327c); [p-5df281](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-5df281).

[^9]: Full chapters: *Andreas* [ch. 1](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/); *Elene* [ch. 1](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/); *Juliana* [ch. 1](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/).
